subsystem: libpng PNG chunk reader — png_handle_PLTE(), the handler that parses the PLTE (palette) critical chunk of a PNG datastream. It validates chunk ordering, reads up to 256 RGB palette entries into a fixed stack array, and installs them via png_set_PLTE().
provenance: Identified by the libpng naming conventions and API: png_structrp / png_inforp opaque handle typedefs, png_uint_32 fixed-width types, the png_color / png_colorp palette entry type, the PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH / PNG_COLOR_TYPE_PALETTE / PNG_COLOR_MASK_COLOR / PNG_HAVE_IHDR / PNG_HAVE_PLTE / PNG_HAVE_IDAT constants, and helper calls png_crc_read/png_crc_finish/png_crc_error/png_chunk_error/png_chunk_benign_error/png_set_PLTE. The function name png_handle_PLTE and the PNG_READ_OPT_PLTE_SUPPORTED / PNG_POINTER_INDEXING_SUPPORTED / PNG_READ_tRNS_SUPPORTED build macros are libpng's pngrutil.c. The header comment 'sample: primevul_c_train_2622_vuln' is an external label and is treated as untrusted metadata, not as ground truth.
trust_boundary: Everything this function consumes originates from a PNG file/stream, which is fully attacker-controlled. The png_uint_32 'length' parameter is the PLTE chunk's declared data length taken straight off the wire. png_crc_read() pulls the chunk body bytes (the palette RGB triples) from the same untrusted stream. png_ptr->color_type, png_ptr->bit_depth and png_ptr->mode were parsed from an earlier IHDR chunk that is equally attacker-controlled. Therefore 'length', the palette bytes, color_type and bit_depth are all hostile inputs; the only trusted element is the fixed compile-time constant PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH (256 in libpng).

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the PLTE chunk handler. It receives the read-context handle png_ptr, the destination info handle info_ptr, and 'length' — the attacker-declared byte length of the PLTE chunk body. The critical local is 'palette', a fixed-size stack array of PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH (256) png_color structures; every palette entry read from the file lands here. 'max_palette_length' will hold the maximum number of entries permitted for the image's bit depth, 'num' the number of entries actually read, and 'i' the loop counter. pal_ptr is an optional cursor used only when pointer-indexing is compiled in. The whole security question of this function is whether 'num' can ever exceed 256, because that is the size of this stack buffer.

A debug trace, then the first state gate: PLTE is only meaningful after IHDR has been seen. If the PNG_HAVE_IHDR bit is not set in png_ptr->mode, png_chunk_error() is invoked, which for a critical chunk is a fatal error (it longjmps / aborts the read). This guarantees that by the time the parsing arithmetic below runs, color_type and bit_depth have been populated from IHDR.

Two more ordering gates. If PNG_HAVE_PLTE is already set, a second PLTE is a fatal 'duplicate' error (the spec forbids more than one PLTE). If IDAT has already been seen, the PLTE is out of place; here png_crc_finish(png_ptr, length) consumes and CRC-checks the remaining 'length' bytes of the chunk so the stream stays aligned, a benign error is raised, and the function returns without touching the palette buffer. Note that in the out-of-place branch 'length' is used purely to skip bytes, so no bound on it is needed there.

The HAVE_PLTE bit is set so a later duplicate can be detected. Then, if the color type has no COLOR mask bit (i.e. it is a grayscale image), a PLTE is meaningless: the chunk bytes are drained with png_crc_finish(png_ptr, length), a benign error is issued, and the function returns. Again 'length' is only used to skip, so its magnitude is harmless here.

When the optional-PLTE feature is NOT compiled in, a PLTE on a truecolor (RGB/RGBA) image is unnecessary; the library skips the chunk body via png_crc_finish and returns. This branch means that in a minimal build only genuine PALETTE images proceed to the read loop; in a full build (OPT_PLTE supported) RGB images also proceed and get the palette read as an optional suggested quantization palette.

This is the central length-validation gate and the reason the fixed stack buffer is safe. The chunk must contain whole RGB triples (length % 3 == 0) and must not exceed 3*PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH == 768 bytes, i.e. 256 entries. If either condition fails the chunk is drained and rejected — fatally for a real palette image, benignly for an RGB image. After this guard, length is guaranteed to be in {0,3,6,...,768}, so length/3 <= 256 == PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH. This is the invariant that keeps the read loop inside the 256-entry palette[] array.

max_palette_length is the number of entries actually usable for this image: for a true PALETTE image it is 1<<bit_depth (2, 4, 16, or 256 for bit depths 1/2/4/8), and for an RGB image treated with an optional palette it is the full 256. 'num' is the number of triples present in the chunk, length/3, which the prior guard has bounded to <= 256; the cast to int is therefore safe as the comment notes. Note that 1<<bit_depth for a corrupted bit_depth of 16 would be 65536, but that only enlarges max_palette_length and never lifts num above 256, so it cannot enlarge the write count.

This truncation is the security-relevant fix for the bit-depth/palette-size mismatch (the CVE-2015-8126 family). If the chunk carries more entries than the bit depth can address (e.g. a 1-bit image with a 200-entry palette), 'num' is clamped down to max_palette_length so that neither the read loop nor the downstream png_set_PLTE()/png_get_PLTE() sees a palette count larger than the bit depth supports. Because num was already <= 256 from the length gate, this clamp can only lower it further; the clamp protects downstream consumers that index the palette by pixel value, not the local buffer (which the length gate already protects).

Two equivalent implementations of the actual read: 'num' times, three bytes are pulled from the stream into a local buf[3] via png_crc_read (which also folds them into the running CRC), then copied into the red/green/blue members of palette entry i. The pointer-indexing variant walks pal_ptr from &palette[0]; the array variant indexes palette[i] and deliberately avoids assuming png_color field order. Both write exactly 'num' entries, and num <= 256 == PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH, so the writes stay within the 256-entry stack array. Each png_crc_read of 3 bytes into a 3-byte buffer is exact-sized.

After the read, any unread tail bytes of the chunk must be consumed to keep the stream aligned and complete the CRC. That tail count is length - num*3: it is 0 when the whole chunk was read (num == length/3 and length%3 == 0), and positive when num was clamped below length/3 by the bit-depth truncation, in which case the surplus entries are skipped. Because num <= length/3, this difference is always >= 0. For a palette image the CRC is always finished; for an RGB image the alternate branch below decides.

For an RGB image where PLTE is optional (ancillary in spirit), CRC failures are handled per the ancillary-CRC policy flags rather than by aborting: depending on PNG_FLAG_CRC_ANCILLARY_USE and PNG_FLAG_CRC_ANCILLARY_NOWARN the code silently returns, raises a CRC error, or emits a warning and continues to use the palette. This block is policy/plumbing around CRC handling and does not itself touch the palette buffer bounds.

The parsed palette (the stack array) and its entry count 'num' are handed to png_set_PLTE(), which copies them into png_ptr/info_ptr's own storage for later pixel decoding. This is the point where 'num' crosses into the rest of the library; the correctness of the earlier length gate and the bit-depth clamp is what makes the count passed here trustworthy. The TODO comment above warns about an unrelated aliasing/ownership quirk between the png_ptr and info_ptr palette copies, not a memory-safety bug in this function.

Final ordering enforcement: the tRNS, hIST and bKGD chunks must appear after PLTE. If a tRNS was already seen (num_trans>0 or the info valid flag set) it is cancelled by zeroing num_trans in both handles — deliberately without clearing the valid flag so duplicate detection still works — and a benign error is raised. Similar benign errors flag out-of-order hIST and bKGD. Each info_ptr access here is NULL-guarded (info_ptr != NULL) before dereference, so these checks are safe.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This 256-entry fixed stack array is the destination of an attacker-controlled, length-prefixed copy, so it is the classic CWE-120 stack-buffer surface. The only thing that keeps it safe is the guard at line 50 (length > 3*PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH => reject) which bounds num = length/3 to <= 256 before the read loop at lines 76-96 writes 'num' entries. The invariant the code must uphold is: num can never exceed PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH. In this revision that invariant is enforced, so there is no overflow; the risk is latent — if the length check at line 50 were ever weakened, removed, or the constant PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH diverged from the array dimension, the read loop would write past palette[255] and smash the stack (CWE-787). Confirm PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH is exactly the array bound and that no code path reaches the loop bypassing line 50.

This is the anchor of the CVE-2015-8126 class: a palette whose entry count exceeds what the IHDR bit depth can address. Attacker controls both bit_depth (from IHDR) and the PLTE length, so a 1-bit image (max 2 entries) with a 256-entry PLTE is trivial to craft. The clamp num = max_palette_length is the fix — it prevents a too-large 'num' from reaching png_set_PLTE() and, later, png_get_PLTE()/pixel decoding where an oversized palette count historically drove out-of-bounds reads/writes. Two sub-hazards to note: (1) 1 << png_ptr->bit_depth is a shift by an attacker-influenced value; for the valid palette bit depths 1/2/4/8 it is fine, but if bit_depth were ever a large or negative value (>= 32) this would be undefined behavior — the safety here rests on IHDR having already rejected illegal bit depths for the color type. (2) The clamp only ever lowers num, so it cannot by itself overflow the local buffer; its value is protecting downstream consumers. Confirm IHDR validation constrains bit_depth to {1,2,4,8} for PALETTE images before this shift executes.

The read loop copies attacker bytes into successive palette entries. Its safety is entirely a function of the loop bound 'num'; png_crc_read itself moves a fixed 3 bytes into a fixed 3-byte buffer, so there is no per-iteration overflow. The failure mode to guard against is any future change that lets 'num' or the pal_ptr cursor advance past palette[255]. Because num <= 256 here, pal_ptr reaches at most &palette[256] (one-past-end) only after the final valid write to palette[255], so no out-of-bounds store occurs. The invariant: the loop count must equal the clamped, length-bounded 'num' and nothing may reset it inside the loop.

This computes the number of unread tail bytes to skip. The expression mixes a png_uint_32 'length' cast to int with num*3. It is safe in this revision because length <= 768 (fits in int with no sign issue) and num <= length/3 guarantees length - num*3 >= 0. The latent concern (CWE-191 style) is that this is a signed subtraction of two attacker-influenced quantities: if a future edit let num*3 exceed length (e.g. num clamped up instead of down, or the line-50 bound loosened), the argument would go negative and png_crc_finish would receive a bogus/huge unsigned count, potentially over-reading the stream or corrupting CRC state. Confirm num*3 <= length is an enforced invariant wherever this line is reached.

This hands the palette and 'num' to png_set_PLTE(), whose implementation is not shown. The latent contract lives there: png_set_PLTE must itself validate num against PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH and against the image bit depth before copying, because historically (CVE-2015-8126) png_set_PLTE/png_get_PLTE trusted the caller's count and overran their internal palette storage when a small bit depth was combined with a large palette. Here the caller has already clamped num (line 73-74), so the call site is well-behaved, but a reviewer must confirm png_set_PLTE does not re-derive an unclamped entry count or allocate based on num without bounding it. Impact if that downstream guard is missing: out-of-bounds read/write during later pixel decode (CWE-125/CWE-787).

All of the bounds reasoning depends on png_ptr->color_type and png_ptr->bit_depth being populated and validated by prior IHDR processing. This gate only checks that IHDR was seen (the HAVE_IHDR bit), not that its fields are self-consistent. If IHDR handling elsewhere failed to reject an out-of-range bit_depth for a PALETTE color type, the 1 << png_ptr->bit_depth at line 64 and the whole max_palette_length logic could behave unexpectedly. This is a cross-function trust dependency (CWE-20): confirm that the IHDR handler rejects illegal color_type/bit_depth combinations so that the values consumed here are already sane.

summary: This is the hardened/patched revision of libpng's png_handle_PLTE. The single fixed stack buffer palette[256] is protected by an explicit length gate (length > 3*PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH || length % 3 -> reject) that bounds the write count to <= 256, and the bit-depth-vs-palette-size mismatch that caused CVE-2015-8126 is mitigated by the num = max_palette_length clamp. No exploitable memory-safety bug is present in the shown code; the findings are low-severity latent contracts (the buffer's dependence on the line-50 guard, the downstream png_set_PLTE count contract, and a signed tail-length subtraction) that must remain intact and are worth confirming in code the header only calls into.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-size stack buffer that is the destination of an attacker-length-driven copy; per method a fixed buffer paired with a length field must get a finding (currently bounded/safe).
Attacker-controlled length/count field driving num and the copy loop; captured by the palette-buffer and tail-subtraction findings.
This is the validating guard itself; it correctly bounds length to <= 768 and enforces whole-triple alignment before any read — it is the mitigation, not a defect.
Attacker-controlled bit_depth drives a shift and the palette-size limit; central to the CVE-2015-8126 class (mitigated here by the clamp).
Cast is safe because line 50 already bounds length < 3*256; num <= 256 so no overflow or sign issue.
The security-relevant clamp protecting downstream palette consumers from a bit-depth/size mismatch; noted as the CVE-2015-8126 mitigation.
Attacker-byte copy into palette entries; bounded by num but is the write site whose safety depends on the num invariant.
Signed subtraction of two attacker-influenced quantities; safe today (num*3 <= length) but a latent CWE-191 if the invariant is broken.
Hands num across a trust boundary into unseen code; latent contract that png_set_PLTE/png_get_PLTE must also bound the count (CVE-2015-8126).
Establish trust in color_type/bit_depth; the IHDR gate only checks presence, creating a cross-function validation dependency (captured as a low finding).
Pure early-out that drains the chunk; uses length only to skip bytes, no buffer contract.
Compile-time feature gate that skips the chunk for RGB images; no memory contract.
CRC policy plumbing for ancillary treatment; does not touch buffer bounds.
Documents a palette ownership/aliasing quirk between png_ptr and info_ptr, an API surprise not a memory-safety bug.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: tRNS/hIST/bKGD ordering checks with info_ptr access (lines 157-182)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Every info_ptr dereference is NULL-guarded (info_ptr != NULL) and only clears counts/raises benign errors; no unsafe access.

findings.title: Fixed 256-entry palette stack buffer depends on the PLTE length gate to bound the copy
findings.bug_class: Stack buffer overflow (length field vs fixed buffer)
findings.invariant: The number of entries written by the read loop (num) must never exceed PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH (256), which requires the guard at line 50 (length > 3*PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH || length % 3 -> reject) to run before the loop on every path, and PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH to equal the array dimension exactly.
findings.failure_mode: If line 50 were weakened/removed or bypassed, an attacker supplying a PLTE chunk longer than 768 bytes would drive num = length/3 above 256, and the loop at lines 76-96 would write png_color entries past palette[255], overflowing the stack (escalating to CWE-787). In the code as shown the guard is present and correct, so this is a latent/defensive finding — the buffer is safe today but its safety is wholly delegated to that one check.
findings.what_to_confirm: That PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH is exactly the array bound (256), that no execution path reaches the read loop without first passing the line-50 length/modulo guard, and that num is never modified inside the loop.

findings.title: Palette-count vs bit-depth mismatch (CVE-2015-8126 class) mitigated only by the num clamp
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds access from oversized palette relative to bit depth (downstream)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0252_cwe120.c (mitigation) with the latent hazard in png_set_PLTE/png_get_PLTE — implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The palette entry count handed downstream must not exceed the number of entries addressable by the image bit depth (1<<bit_depth for PALETTE images); IHDR must have already restricted bit_depth to {1,2,4,8} for PALETTE color type so that 1 << png_ptr->bit_depth is well-defined.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted PNG with a small IHDR bit depth (e.g. 1) plus an oversized PLTE historically let too many palette entries reach png_set_PLTE/png_get_PLTE and overran their palette storage or was over-read during pixel decode (CVE-2015-8126). The clamp at lines 73-74 fixes this by lowering num to max_palette_length before line 146. Residual concern: 1 << png_ptr->bit_depth is a shift by an attacker-influenced value that is only safe because IHDR is assumed to have rejected illegal bit depths; a bit_depth >= 32 would be undefined behavior.
findings.what_to_confirm: That IHDR validation constrains bit_depth to legal values for PALETTE images before this shift, and that png_set_PLTE/png_get_PLTE also bound the entry count independently rather than trusting the caller.

findings.title: Signed tail-length subtraction (int)length - num*3 can go negative if the num invariant breaks
findings.bug_class: Signed/integer underflow feeding a byte-skip count
findings.invariant: num*3 must always be <= length so that the tail count length - num*3 is non-negative; this holds because num <= length/3 (num is at most length/3 and only ever clamped downward).
findings.failure_mode: The argument is a signed subtraction of two attacker-influenced values. Today it is safe (length <= 768 and num <= length/3), so the result is 0..length. If a future change clamped num upward, loosened the line-50 bound, or altered the arithmetic so that num*3 > length, the expression would become negative and png_crc_finish would receive a bogus (potentially huge when reinterpreted) skip count, over-reading the stream or corrupting CRC/stream position. No live bug in the shown revision.
findings.what_to_confirm: That num*3 <= length is guaranteed on every path reaching line 108, and that png_crc_finish treats its length argument as this bounded remaining-byte count.

findings.title: Palette count crosses trust boundary into png_set_PLTE, which must re-bound it
findings.bug_class: Unvalidated count passed to callee (latent OOB in downstream consumer)
findings.invariant: png_set_PLTE (and later png_get_PLTE / pixel decode) must treat 'num' as bounded by PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH and by the bit depth, and must not allocate/copy/index based on an unclamped re-derived count.
findings.failure_mode: This is the exit point where the parsed count leaves the reviewed function. Because the caller has already clamped num, the call is well-behaved here, but the memory safety of the stored palette ultimately depends on png_set_PLTE not re-trusting an oversized count — the exact failure that produced CVE-2015-8126. If png_set_PLTE allocates by num without bounding, or later code indexes the palette by pixel value beyond num, out-of-bounds reads/writes occur during decode.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect png_set_PLTE to confirm it bounds/validates 'num' against PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH before copying and that subsequent pixel decode never indexes past the stored entry count.

audit_checklist: Confirm PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH == 256 and equals the exact dimension of the palette[] array at line 4.
Confirm every path to the read loop (lines 76-96) first passes the line-50 guard (length > 3*PNG_MAX_PALETTE_LENGTH || length % 3) so num <= 256.
Confirm IHDR handling rejects illegal bit_depth for PALETTE color type so that 1 << png_ptr->bit_depth (line 64) is well-defined and never shifts by >= 32.
Confirm the clamp at lines 73-74 (num = max_palette_length) executes before png_set_PLTE and that num is never re-widened afterward.
Confirm (int)length - num*3 at line 108 is always >= 0 (i.e. num*3 <= length on every reaching path).
Inspect png_set_PLTE/png_get_PLTE to confirm they independently bound the palette entry count (CVE-2015-8126 mitigation) rather than trusting the caller's num.
Confirm png_crc_read always transfers exactly 3 bytes into buf[3] and cannot write more than requested.
Confirm all info_ptr dereferences (lines 158-181) remain NULL-guarded.
